cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 22-year-old presents with sudden shortness of breath, tingling fingers, and chest tightness during emotional distress.

questions_flow.prompt: Is the patient acidemic, alkalemic, or normal?

cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~21.8; measured 22.1 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 67-year-old presents with dizziness and volume depletion after recurrent nausea and retching.

questions_flow.prompt: Is the patient acidemic, alkalemic, or normal?

cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~47.0; measured 46.9 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 35-year-old presents with weakness and dehydration after ongoing gastrointestinal fluid loss.

questions_flow.prompt: Is the patient acidemic, alkalemic, or normal?

cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~50.5; measured 50.2 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.

cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 55-year-old is brought to ED febrile, hypotensive, and hypotensive.

questions_flow.prompt: Is the patient acidemic, alkalemic, or normal?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes respiratory alkalosis due to hyperventilation.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 65-year-old with chronic respiratory symptoms presents with worsening shortness of breath over several days.

questions_flow.prompt: Is the patient acidemic, alkalemic, or normal?

cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~32.4; measured 32.6 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: 65-year-old is brought in unwell with tachycardic, febrile, and febrile.

questions_flow.prompt: Is the patient acidemic, alkalemic, or normal?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes high anion gap metabolic acidosis due to lactate accumulation.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.clinical_stem: 27-year-old presents with nausea, hyperventilation, and tinnitus after ingesting a large quantity of tablets.

questions_flow.prompt: Is the patient acidemic, alkalemic, or normal?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (137 − (101 + 16) = 20) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: 65-year-old is admitted with confusion, with rigors, and with rigors.

questions_flow.prompt: Is the patient acidemic, alkalemic, or normal?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes high anion gap metabolic acidosis due to lactate accumulation.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 27-year-old attends ED with with abdominal pain and with reduced oral intake.

questions_flow.prompt: Is the patient acidemic, alkalemic, or normal?

cases.explanation: Low pH = acidemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winter’s formula predicts PaCO2 ~23.0 (±2); measured 22.6 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 135 − (102 + 10) = 23 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

cases.clinical_stem: 55-year-old is brought to ED with with pneumonia and tachycardic.

questions_flow.prompt: Is the patient acidemic, alkalemic, or normal?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes respiratory alkalosis due to hyperventilation.

questions_flow.prompt: Is the patient acidemic, alkalemic, or normal?

cases.explanation: COPD exacerbations cause acute rises in CO₂ on a background of chronic respiratory acidosis.

cases.clinical_stem: 34-year-old presents dehydrated and weak after several days of high-output gastrointestinal losses.

questions_flow.prompt: Is the patient acidemic, alkalemic, or normal?

cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidemia. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter’s formula predicts PaCO2 ~36.5 (±2); measured 37.4 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 140 − (113 + 19) = 8, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.
